Daily DAP Accessibility Report — 2026-04-18#

Run ID: run-2026-04-18-e3bd992529 · Status: success · Source data: 2026-04-16 · Generated: 2026-04-18T09:34:24.675Z

View HTML/CSS/JS Code Quality report for this date →

About These Reports#

The Digital Analytics Program (DAP) is a U.S. government analytics service that collects website traffic data across participating federal agencies. DAP tracks page views, visitor counts, and usage patterns for hundreds of government websites, providing transparency into how the public engages with federal digital services. The traffic data is publicly available at analytics.usa.gov.

This report measures the quality and accessibility of the top 100 most-visited U.S. government URLs as reported by DAP. Each day, Lighthouse scans are run against these URLs to measure:

  • Performance - How fast pages load for users (scores 0-100, higher is better)
  • Accessibility - How well pages work for users with disabilities, following WCAG guidelines (scores 0-100, higher is better)
  • Best Practices - Whether pages follow modern web development standards (scores 0-100, higher is better)
  • SEO - How well pages are optimized for search engines (scores 0-100, higher is better)

Accessibility findings come from axe-core, the industry-standard accessibility testing engine embedded in Lighthouse. The axe findings surface specific WCAG violations such as missing alternative text, insufficient color contrast, and missing form labels that make government websites harder to use for people with disabilities.

Traffic data reflects daily visitor counts from DAP. URLs are ranked by page load count, ensuring the most-used government pages are prioritized for quality measurement.

Accessibility Trend Narrative#

Over the past 30 days of data (2026-03-19 to 2026-04-17), government website accessibility scores have remained stable (-0.44 points, from 92.8 to 92.36). Performance scores have improved (+1.3 points, from 54.03 to 55.33).

Today's aggregate accessibility score of 93.54 reflects the mean Lighthouse accessibility score across 99 successfully scanned government URLs. A score above 90 indicates generally strong compliance with WCAG automated checks, though manual testing is always recommended to fully assess accessibility.

Common Accessibility Issues (Top 10)#

The following axe-core rules were most frequently violated across scanned URLs today. These patterns indicate systemic accessibility barriers present across multiple government websites.

Human impact narratives for top issues

The following narratives describe the real-world impact of each accessibility barrier on people with disabilities. These barriers are not abstract technical failures; they prevent citizens from independently accessing government services.

heading-order: Document Structure Navigation

Screen reader users navigate complex government websites primarily through heading structure, using headings as a table of contents to jump between sections. Skipped heading levels break the logical document outline, causing confusion about the hierarchy of information and forcing users to re-read sections to understand the relationship between topics, adding significant time and effort to information-gathering tasks.

Affected groups:

  • People who are blind using screen readers
  • People who are deaf and rely on visual-to-text tools
  • People with motor disabilities using keyboard navigation

target-size: Digital Motor Access

Small touch targets act as a digital gatekeeper, excluding individuals with tremors, arthritis, or limited dexterity from accessing essential services independently. These technical failures transform a routine interaction into a source of failure, stripping away the autonomy of citizens who require a frictionless, accessible interface to participate in digital life. The approximately 58 million Americans with ambulatory or self-care disabilities are disproportionately impacted by inadequate touch target sizing on government mobile websites.

Affected groups:

  • People with Parkinson's disease, arthritis, or hand tremors
  • Older adults with reduced fine motor control
  • People with motor disabilities using alternative pointing devices
  • People in situational impairment contexts (e.g., commuting, holding a child)

landmark-one-main: Primary Content Identification

Without a main landmark, screen reader users lose their most efficient way to skip to the primary content of a government page. Every page visit requires tabbing through the entire navigation structure to reach the actual content, a significant time and effort burden that accumulates across repeated visits to government websites for citizens managing ongoing benefit cases or legal matters.

Affected groups:

  • People who are blind using screen readers
  • People who are deaf and rely on visual-to-text tools
  • People with motor disabilities using keyboard navigation

color-contrast: Visual Information Access

Low contrast text is one of the most pervasive barriers on government websites, rendering critical information invisible to the approximately 26 million Americans with low vision or color blindness. When agency announcements, form instructions, error messages, or legal notices are displayed in insufficient contrast, affected citizens are denied equal access to the information they need to exercise their rights and access public services.

Affected groups:

  • People with low vision including age-related vision loss
  • People who are color blind (approximately 8% of men, 0.5% of women)

label-content-name-mismatch: Voice Control Activation

When the accessible name of an element does not match its visible text, voice control users cannot activate it using the words they see on screen. Saying "click Submit" to a button whose visual label is "Submit" but whose accessible name is "submitbtn" results in no action. This mismatch silently breaks voice control for citizens with motor disabilities who depend on this input method to interact with government websites.

Affected groups:

  • People who are blind using screen readers
  • People with low vision relying on screen magnifiers
  • People who are deaf using keyboard navigation
  • People with motor disabilities using voice control software

link-name: Link Purpose Clarity

Links without accessible names are completely useless to screen reader users who navigate government pages by jumping between links. An unnamed link could lead anywhere, and activating it unknowingly could trigger downloads, open unexpected pages, or initiate unintended processes. On government websites, unnamed links undermine the informed consent principle by preventing citizens from knowing where a link will take them.

Affected groups:

  • People who are blind using screen readers
  • People who are deaf and rely on visual-to-text tools
  • People with motor disabilities using keyboard navigation

tabindex: Focus Order Predictability

Positive tabindex values create unpredictable focus sequences that jump around the page in ways that differ from the visual reading order. For keyboard users and screen reader users on government websites, an erratic tab order makes it difficult to reliably navigate forms and interactive content, increasing errors and the time needed to complete tasks, and creating particular frustration for users with cognitive disabilities who need a predictable, logical interaction flow.

Affected groups:

  • People who are blind using screen readers
  • People who are deaf using keyboard-only navigation
  • People with motor disabilities using keyboard or switch access

listitem: List Item Semantic Context

Orphaned list items outside of list containers lose their semantic meaning and list navigation properties. Screen reader users rely on list structure to understand that a set of items is related and to count the total number of items. Government navigation menus, step lists, and option groups presented as invalid lists are harder to parse and navigate efficiently.

Affected groups:

  • People who are blind using screen readers
  • People who are deaf and rely on visual-to-text tools
  • People with motor disabilities navigating by keyboard

aria-required-children: Widget Structure Integrity

ARIA composite widgets without the correct child roles create structurally broken interfaces for screen reader users. Menus without menu items, lists without list items, and grids without cells all degrade to incoherent structures, making navigation impossible and forcing blind users to abandon complex interactive government tools.

Affected groups:

  • People who are blind using screen readers
  • People who are deaf and rely on visual-to-text tools
  • People with motor disabilities navigating by keyboard

button-name: Button Discoverability

Unlabeled buttons are silent black holes in the government digital experience. When a screen reader announces a button with no name, blind users cannot know what action it will perform. Submit, cancel, search, and navigation buttons without accessible names prevent citizens from completing applications, accessing benefits, or finding essential government resources independently.

Affected groups:

  • People who are blind using screen readers
  • People who are deaf and rely on visual-to-text tools
Disability icon key
Without Vision — People who are blind or have no functional vision (1.0% of U.S. population — ~3,400,000 Americans)
Limited Vision — People with low vision who need magnification or high contrast (2.4% of U.S. population — ~8,100,000 Americans)
Without Perception of Color — People who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness) (4.3% of U.S. population — ~14,500,000 Americans)
Without Hearing — People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content (0.3% of U.S. population — ~1,100,000 Americans)
Limited Hearing — People with hearing loss who may struggle with audio without accommodations (3.5% of U.S. population — ~11,900,000 Americans)
Without Speech — People who cannot use speech or voice-based input effectively (0.5% of U.S. population — ~1,700,000 Americans)
Limited Manipulation — People with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity (2.2% of U.S. population — ~7,600,000 Americans)
Limited Reach and Strength — People with limited reach, strength, or stamina (5.8% of U.S. population — ~19,600,000 Americans)
Limited Language, Cognitive, and Learning Abilities — People with cognitive, learning, or language differences (4.7% of U.S. population — ~15,900,000 Americans)

These icons show which groups of people with disabilities are excluded by each accessibility barrier. Where page view data is available, each icon shows an estimated number of people potentially excluded (page loads for affected URLs × disability prevalence rate from U.S. Census ACS 2023). Hover over or focus an icon to see the full estimate and methodology. Icons follow the Section 508 Functional Performance Criteria and the equivalent EU EN 301 549 v3.2.1 Table B.2 categories.

Prevalence data: U.S. Census Bureau, American Community Survey (ACS) 2023 1-Year Estimates, Table B18101; supplemental data from CDC, NIDCD, AFB, and NIH/NEI. U.S. population base: ~336,500,000 (2023 estimate). Reviewed annually.

See the Section 508 WCAG to FPC mapping for additional detail on how accessibility requirements map to functional needs.

Usability Heuristics Summary#

The most common accessibility violations map to Nielsen Norman Group's 10 usability heuristics. This table shows which heuristics are most affected by today's top axe-core violations, helping to identify where systemic usability barriers exist.

Americans Excluded by Disability Today#

Based on 17,022,412 page loads across successfully scanned government URLs and U.S. disability prevalence rates from the U.S. Census Bureau, American Community Survey (ACS) 2023 1-Year Estimates, Table B18101 (2023), an estimated 1,120,000 Americans encountered an accessibility barrier on a government website today.

Each row shows the number of people in a disability group who visited a page with at least one accessibility issue that affects their group. Prevalence rates and U.S. population estimates are derived from the American Community Survey (ACS) and supplemental sources. These figures are rough estimates intended to illustrate the scale of accessibility barriers.

Estimated Americans excluded today by disability category (2023 Census data)

Limited Manipulation 2.2% ~7,600,000 11,100,000 240,000
Without Perception of Color 4.3% ~14,500,000 4,430,000 190,000
Limited Vision 2.4% ~8,100,000 5,750,000 130,000
Without Vision 1.0% ~3,400,000 11,300,000 110,000
Without Hearing 0.3% ~1,100,000 11,300,000 34,053
Limited Language, Cognitive, and Learning Abilities 4.7% ~15,900,000 160,000 7,544

Sources: U.S. Census Bureau, American Community Survey (ACS) 2023 1-Year Estimates, Table B18101; disability prevalence from ACS 2023 and supplemental CDC/NIDCD/NIH data. Reviewed annually.

Performance Impact on Americans#

Google defines a good Largest Contentful Paint (LCP) as under 2.5 seconds and recommends pages under 1.6 MB. The figures below estimate how much extra time Americans spend waiting, and how much extra data is transferred, because government websites fall short of these benchmarks. Calculations are based on 97 successfully scanned URLs with Lighthouse timing data.

Estimated performance impact vs. Google benchmarks for government URLs scanned on April 18, 2026
Metric Estimated total (April 18, 2026) Notes
Extra time waiting (vs 2.5s LCP benchmark) 2 years, 5 months, 26 daysExtra time is calculated as: for each scanned URL, max(0, actual LCP − 2.5s) × page loads. Total: 78,532,482 seconds. 78,532,482 seconds
Extra data transferred (vs 1.6 MB page weight benchmark) ~15.3 TB (636 copies of Wikipedia)Extra data is calculated as: for each scanned URL, max(0, actual page weight − 1.6 MB) × page loads. Across 97 URLs with weight data

LCP and page weight are measured by Lighthouse. Wikipedia copy count uses a size of 24.05 GB per Wikipedia:Size of Wikipedia. These are rough estimates based on a sample of the top government URLs by traffic. Hover or focus the estimated totals for calculation details.

Content Density (Words per Megabyte)#

Words-per-Megabyte (WpM) measures how efficiently a page delivers readable content relative to its total download size. A low ratio indicates Digital Bloat—the page transfers far more data than the text it contains. Pages below 200 WpM are flagged for optimization review.

  • URLs with content metrics: 67
  • Average word count: 231
  • Average Words/MB: 106
  • URLs below 200 WpM threshold: 57
Content density for top URLs scanned on April 18, 2026 (up to 10 shown)
URL Word Count Words/MB

https://collections.nlm.nih.gov 998 251
https://va.gov 322 147
https://usajobs.gov 174 142
https://cdc.gov 242 77

Word counts are extracted from the page’s main article content using Mozilla Readability, which strips navigation, sidebars, and ads. Page weight is the total resource size measured by Lighthouse. Pages below 200 WpM may impose unnecessary data costs on users on metered connections.

Detected Technologies#

Technology signals detected from the network requests loaded by each scanned URL. CMS detection identifies WordPress, Drupal, Joomla, ASP.NET, SharePoint, Squarespace, Adobe Experience Manager, and TYPO3 from characteristic asset paths. USWDS detection identifies use of the U.S. Web Design System.

CMS platform counts and average Lighthouse scores (where available) across 99 successfully scanned URLs
CMS URLs Share Avg AccessibilityAvg PerformanceAvg Best PracticesAvg SEO
Drupal 30Sites: acquisition.gov, archives.gov, cbp.gov, cisa.gov, cms.gov, dhs.gov, disasterassistance.gov, epa.gov, faa.gov, fdic.gov, flag.dol.gov, fs.usda.gov, gsa.gov, ice.gov, justice.gov, medicare.gov, msc.fema.gov, ofac.treasury.gov, ornl.gov, osha.gov, sam.gov, sba.gov, studentaid.gov, tsa.gov, usa.gov, uscis.gov, usgs.gov, usitc.gov, uspto.gov, www.hud.gov 30% 95 45 81 92
WordPress 5Sites: nasa.gov, news.va.gov, science.nasa.gov, state.gov, vltfusion.org 5% 93 55 75 92
Adobe Experience Manager 2Sites: travel.state.gov, water.noaa.gov 2% 91 41 73 78
ASP.NET 2Sites: ipps-a.army.mil, news.uscg.mil 2% 88 61 79 100

USWDS detected on 34 of 99 scanned URLs (34%).

Observed USWDS versions: 2.9.0Sites: fbi.gov, 3.8.0Sites: fdic.gov. The latest release is available at github.com/uswds/uswds/releases.

Third-Party JavaScript Services#

Third-party scripts detected from network requests loaded by scanned pages. These services may affect page performance, user privacy, and security posture.

12 services marked ⚠ tracking send user data to third-party servers and may have privacy implications for site visitors.

Accessibility Statements (M-24-08)#

OMB Memorandum M-24-08 requires each federal agency to publish a digital accessibility statement that includes contact information for accessibility issues, known limitations, and a link to the agency Section 508 program page.

42 of 99 domains (42%) checked have a detectable accessibility statement at a standard URL path.

Required Federal Links (M-17-06)#

OMB Memorandum M-17-06 and the 21st Century IDEA Act require federal agency public websites to provide links to a Privacy Policy, Contact page, and FOIA page. These were among the compliance criteria benchmarked by the performance.gov federal website performance initiative (no longer active, but the underlying requirements remain in force).

59 of 99 domains (60%) have all three required link types detectable at standard URL paths.

Section 508: Legal Requirements vs. Best Practices#

Section 508 of the Rehabilitation Act was enacted in 1998 and requires U.S. federal agencies to make their electronic and information technology accessible to people with disabilities. The technical standards were significantly updated through the ICT Accessibility Standards refresh, which became effective in January 2018 — the same year Apple celebrated the 10th anniversary of the iPhone. That refresh incorporated WCAG 2.0 Level AA (published in 2008) as the baseline for web content.

The web has changed dramatically since then. What agencies are legally required to meet today is built on a standard from 2008 — the same era as the original iPhone. Agencies should treat WCAG 2.0 AA as the floor, not the ceiling.

Legal Requirement

WCAG 2.0 Level AA (published 2008)

Referenced by the Section 508 ICT Standards refresh (effective 2018). Agencies must meet these 38 success criteria to satisfy their Section 508 obligations for web content.

  • Text alternatives for non-text content
  • Captions and audio descriptions for multimedia
  • Keyboard accessibility and focus management
  • Minimum color contrast (4.5:1 for normal text)
  • No seizure-inducing content
  • Consistent navigation and labeling
  • Error identification and correction

Section 508 web content requirements (E205)

Best Practices

WCAG 2.1 and 2.2 Level AA (published 2018 and 2023)

These later versions add criteria that reflect how the web actually works today — mobile devices, cognitive accessibility, and modern authentication patterns — and are strongly recommended for any agency aiming to serve all Americans. The US Federal Government is one of the last to use the legacy WCAG 2.0 AA Standard, as it was developed ~2 decades ago.

  • WCAG 2.1 (2018): 17 new criteria for mobile, low vision, and cognitive accessibility
  • WCAG 2.2 (2023): 9 more criteria for authentication and cognitive tasks, removing the outdated "Parsing" criterion
  • W3C now recommends WCAG 2.2 as the current normative standard for new and updated content

What is new in WCAG 2.2 · WCAG 2.2 specification

The scores and findings in this report are based on automated checks aligned with WCAG 2.x rules surfaced by axe-core. Passing automated checks is necessary but not sufficient: manual testing with assistive technologies is required to verify true conformance and to catch barriers that automation cannot detect.

Take Action#

Today's scan identified 198 accessibility barriers across the most-visited U.S. government websites, affecting an estimated 1,120,000 Americans with disabilities. Here is how you can help.

  • Read the federal accessibility report. The 2025 Governmentwide Section 508 Assessment from the GSA Administrator details the state of federal accessibility compliance and the steps agencies are taking to improve.
  • Submit URLs you care about. Want specific government pages included in future scans? Submit them to the Open Scans project to help broaden coverage.
  • Test with free tools. Do your own automated and manual accessibility testing with Accessibility Insights and Google Lighthouse. Automated tools catch a significant portion of WCAG failures quickly; manual review with assistive technologies catches the rest.
  • Adopt and update the U.S. Web Design System (USWDS). The USWDS is already a strong foundation for accessible, consistent federal websites, but many agencies have not adopted it or are running outdated versions. Encourage your agency to adopt or upgrade so every American benefits from its accessibility work.
  • Hire people with disabilities. People with disabilities are the ultimate experts on digital barriers. Bringing them into design, engineering, and testing teams — not just as consultants but as full-time employees — leads to more accessible products and services for everyone.

Accessibility Findings

https://tools.usps.com

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 68

Axe findings: 10

`button`, `link`, and `menuitem` elements do not have accessible names. (rule: aria-command-name) DAP-c26fc51b

When an element doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it with a generic name, making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make command elements more accessible.

Disabilities affected: ~27KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~27,657 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 2,765,731 affected page loads) ~8KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~8,297 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 2,765,731 affected page loads) ~60KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~60,846 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 2,765,731 affected page loads)

Affected elements (3):

Element 1 DAP-7ca376ec

Element path: div.panel-heading > h4.panel-title > a#headingOneAnchor > span.down-arr

Snippet:

<span role="button" data-parent="#accordion" aria-expanded="true" aria-controls="collapseOne" class="down-arr">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute

Element 2 DAP-958bc2d3

Element path: div.panel-heading > h4.panel-title > a.collapsed > span.down-arr

Snippet:

<span role="button" data-parent="#accordion" aria-expanded="true" aria-controls="collapseOne" class="down-arr">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute

Element 3 DAP-958bc2d3

Element path: div.panel-heading > h4.panel-title > a.collapsed > span.down-arr

Snippet:

<span role="button" data-parent="#accordion" aria-expanded="true" aria-controls="collapseOne" class="down-arr">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
Elements with an ARIA `[role]` that require children to contain a specific `[role]` are missing some or all of those required children. (rule: aria-required-children) DAP-82af62ae

Some ARIA parent roles must contain specific child roles to perform their intended accessibility functions. Learn more about roles and required children elements.

Element 1 DAP-4f198039

Element path: div#g-navigation > div.nav-full > nav > ul.nav-list

Snippet:

<ul class="nav-list" role="menubar">

Label: Skip Quick Tools Links Quick Tools Track a Package Informed Delivery …

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

`[role]`s are not contained by their required parent element (rule: aria-required-parent) DAP-b319271f

Some ARIA child roles must be contained by specific parent roles to properly perform their intended accessibility functions. Learn more about ARIA roles and required parent element.

Element 1 DAP-b8ca2593

<a aria-expanded="false" role="menuitem" tabindex="0" aria-haspopup="true" class="nav-first-element menuitem" href="#">

Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order) DAP-1abfb3a4

Links do not have a discernible name (rule: link-name) DAP-8c4fed15

Link text (and alternate text for images, when used as links) that is discernible, unique, and focusable improves the navigation experience for screen reader users. Learn how to make links accessible.

Element 1 DAP-2517b980

Element path: div#tracking_page_wrapper > div#g-navigation > div.nav-full > a#endnav

Snippet:

<a name="endnav" id="endnav" href="#" class="hidden-skip">

Label:  

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • Element is in tab order and does not have accessible text
  • Fix any of the following:
  • Element does not have text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute

<a href="https://www.usps.com/" class="global-footer--logo-link">

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • Element is in tab order and does not have accessible text
  • Fix any of the following:
  • Element does not have text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
List items (`<li>`) are not contained within `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` parent elements. (rule: listitem) DAP-dd951b79

Screen readers require list items (`<li>`) to be contained within a parent `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` to be announced properly. Learn more about proper list structure.

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 91

Axe findings: 3

Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order) DAP-b627e7e4

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

Links rely on color to be distinguishable. (rule: link-in-text-block) DAP-817c69a4

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Link text that is discernible improves the experience for users with low vision. Learn how to make links distinguishable.

Estimated ~9,832 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 409,686 affected page loads) ~17KPeople who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Estimated ~17,616 people potentially excluded (4.3% prevalence × 409,686 affected page loads)

Element 1 DAP-25e0050e

Element path: div.fsa-banner-content-body > span > div > a

Snippet:

<a href="/loan-simulator">

Label: explore and apply for other repayment plans

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • The link has insufficient color contrast of 1.64:1 with the surrounding text. (Minimum contrast is 3:1, link text: #0000ee, surrounding text: #212529)
  • The link has no styling (such as underline) to distinguish it from the surrounding text

Element path: div.fsa-banner-content-body > span > div > a

<a href="/announcements-events/idr-court-actions">

  • The link has no styling (such as underline) to distinguish it from the surrounding text

Element path: div.fsa-banner-content-body > span > div > a

Snippet:

<a href="/fsa-id/sign-in/landing/?redirectTo=/my-aid">

Label: Log in

  • The link has no styling (such as underline) to distinguish it from the surrounding text

Element 1 DAP-9bdbf19c

Snippet:

Label: Site Survey

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Target has insufficient size (61px by 23px, should be at least 24px by 24px)
  • Target has insufficient space to its closest neighbors. Safe clickable space has a diameter of 0px instead of at least 24px.

Some elements have a `[tabindex]` value greater than 0 (rule: tabindex) DAP-6eb16f7d

A value greater than 0 implies an explicit navigation ordering. Although technically valid, this often creates frustrating experiences for users who rely on assistive technologies. Learn more about the `tabindex` attribute.

Accessibility Findings

https://tsp.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 91

Axe findings: 2

Buttons do not have an accessible name (rule: button-name) DAP-94f6c92a

When a button doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it as "button", making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make buttons more accessible.

Disabilities affected: ~1KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~1,879 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 187,916 affected page loads) ~564People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~564 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 187,916 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1 DAP-13779943

Element path: aside#tsp-toolbar > ul > li > button#toggle-tsp-zoom

Snippet:

<button id="toggle-tsp-zoom" class="tooltip">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have inner text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element does not have an implicit (wrapped) <label>
  • Element does not have an explicit <label>
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"
Links rely on color to be distinguishable. (rule: link-in-text-block) DAP-c0c32991

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Link text that is discernible improves the experience for users with low vision. Learn how to make links distinguishable.

Accessibility Findings

https://usps.com

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 68

Axe findings: 9

`[aria-*]` attributes do not match their roles (rule: aria-allowed-attr) DAP-4bdf91e9

Each ARIA `role` supports a specific subset of `aria-*` attributes. Mismatching these invalidates the `aria-*` attributes. Learn how to match ARIA attributes to their roles.

Snippet:

<li data-target="#usps-carousel" data-slide-to="2" aria-checked="false">

Element path: div#usps-carousel > div.carousel-inner > ol.carousel-indicators > li

Snippet:

<li data-target="#usps-carousel" data-slide-to="3" aria-checked="false">

Image elements do not have `[alt]` attributes (rule: image-alt) DAP-474d58d2

Informative elements should aim for short, descriptive alternate text. Decorative elements can be ignored with an empty alt attribute. Learn more about the `alt` attribute.

Disabilities affected: ~1KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~1,876 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 187,615 affected page loads) ~563People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~563 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 187,615 affected page loads)

Element 1 DAP-8e1cf442

Element path: body > img

Snippet:

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have an alt attribute
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"
Links do not have a discernible name (rule: link-name) DAP-f1542b28

Link text (and alternate text for images, when used as links) that is discernible, unique, and focusable improves the navigation experience for screen reader users. Learn how to make links accessible.

List items (`<li>`) are not contained within `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` parent elements. (rule: listitem) DAP-2be6feaa

Screen readers require list items (`<li>`) to be contained within a parent `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` to be announced properly. Learn more about proper list structure.

`[user-scalable="no"]` is used in the `<meta name="viewport">` element or the `[maximum-scale]` attribute is less than 5. (rule: meta-viewport) DAP-7b9fc94e

Disabling zooming is problematic for users with low vision who rely on screen magnification to properly see the contents of a web page. Learn more about the viewport meta tag.

Disabilities affected: ~4KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~4,503 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 187,615 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1 DAP-efeb19cc

Element path: head > meta

Snippet:

<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0, maximum-scale=1.0, user-scalable=no">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • user-scalable=no on <meta> tag disables zooming on mobile devices
Touch targets do not have sufficient size or spacing. (rule: target-size) DAP-4f9c3fe1

Touch targets with sufficient size and spacing help users who may have difficulty targeting small controls to activate the targets. Learn more about touch targets.

Estimated ~4,128 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 187,615 affected page loads) ~10KPeople with limited reach, strength, or stamina. Estimated ~10,882 people potentially excluded (5.8% prevalence × 187,615 affected page loads)

Accessibility Findings

https://nasa.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 82

Axe findings: 5

Elements with `role="dialog"` or `role="alertdialog"` do not have accessible names. (rule: aria-dialog-name) DAP-7ed63e03

ARIA dialog elements without accessible names may prevent screen readers users from discerning the purpose of these elements. Learn how to make ARIA dialog elements more accessible.

Affected elements (1):

Element 1 DAP-79d6829d

Element path: body.home > div#pum-872473

Snippet:

<div id="pum-872473" role="dialog" aria-modal="false" class="pum pum-overlay pum-theme-862814 pum-theme-content-only popmake-overlay pu…" data-popmake="{&quot;id&quot;:872473,&quot;slug&quot;:&quot;was-this-page-helpful&quot;,&quot;theme_id&quot;:862814,&quot;cookies&quot;:[{…">

Label: Was this page helpful? Was this page helpful? Yes No ×

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
Buttons do not have an accessible name (rule: button-name) DAP-08b676ee

When a button doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it as "button", making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make buttons more accessible.

Disabilities affected: ~1KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~1,813 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 181,349 affected page loads) ~544People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Background and foreground colors do not have a sufficient contrast ratio. (rule: color-contrast) DAP-c6127178

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Learn how to provide sufficient color contrast.

Disabilities affected: ~4KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~4,352 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 181,349 affected page loads) ~7KPeople who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Estimated ~7,798 people potentially excluded (4.3% prevalence × 181,349 affected page loads)

Snippet:

<input type="submit" id="gform_submit_button_117" class="gform_button button" onclick="gform.submission.handleButtonClick(this);" data-submission-type="submit" value="Submit">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

Links do not have a discernible name (rule: link-name) DAP-73a09313

Link text (and alternate text for images, when used as links) that is discernible, unique, and focusable improves the navigation experience for screen reader users. Learn how to make links accessible.

Disabilities affected: ~1KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~1,813 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 181,349 affected page loads) ~544People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~544 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 181,349 affected page loads) ~3KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~3,990 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 181,349 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1 DAP-0887162f

Element path: div.grid-row > div.grid-col-12 > div.hds-image-download-wrapper > a.hds-image-download-link

Snippet:

<a class="hds-image-download-link" href="https://www.nasa.gov/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/pia26748-spherex-cygnus-h2…">

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • Element is in tab order and does not have accessible text
  • Fix any of the following:
  • Element does not have text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
Touch targets do not have sufficient size or spacing. (rule: target-size) DAP-b6166b68

Touch targets with sufficient size and spacing help users who may have difficulty targeting small controls to activate the targets. Learn more about touch targets.

Elements with visible text labels do not have matching accessible names. (rule: label-content-name-mismatch) DAP-8c268d1c

Visible text labels that do not match the accessible name can result in a confusing experience for screen reader users. Learn more about accessible names.

Disabilities affected: ~1KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~1,743 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 174,291 affected page loads) ~4KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~523 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 174,291 affected page loads) ~3KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~3,834 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 174,291 affected page loads)

Label: FAR Case 2025-007

Accessibility Findings

https://travel.state.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 93

Axe findings: 2

`[user-scalable="no"]` is used in the `<meta name="viewport">` element or the `[maximum-scale]` attribute is less than 5. (rule: meta-viewport) DAP-99efae35

Disabling zooming is problematic for users with low vision who rely on screen magnification to properly see the contents of a web page. Learn more about the viewport meta tag.

Disabilities affected: ~4KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~4,169 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 173,714 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1 DAP-06ade13b

Element path: head > meta

Snippet:

<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0, maximum-scale=1.0, user-scalable=no">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • user-scalable=no on <meta> tag disables zooming on mobile devices
Document does not have a main landmark. (rule: landmark-one-main) DAP-b55de0fc

One main landmark helps screen reader users navigate a web page. Learn more about landmarks.

Disabilities affected: ~1KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~1,737 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 173,714 affected page loads) ~521People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~3,822 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 173,714 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1 DAP-59c242b4

Snippet:

<html lang="en">

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • Document does not have a main landmark

Accessibility Findings

https://ceac.state.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 94

Axe findings: 2

Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order) DAP-58f8b5b4

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

Disabilities affected: ~1KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~1,581 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 158,145 affected page loads) ~474People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~474 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 158,145 affected page loads) ~3KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~3,479 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 158,145 affected page loads)

<h4>

Label: NONIMMIGRANT

  • Heading order invalid
Some elements have a `[tabindex]` value greater than 0 (rule: tabindex) DAP-f0cc6d8d

A value greater than 0 implies an explicit navigation ordering. Although technically valid, this often creates frustrating experiences for users who rely on assistive technologies. Learn more about the `tabindex` attribute.

Disabilities affected: ~1KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~1,581 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 158,145 affected page loads) ~474People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~3,479 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 158,145 affected page loads)

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 97

Elements use prohibited ARIA attributes (rule: aria-prohibited-attr) DAP-bc38b093

Using ARIA attributes in roles where they are prohibited can mean that important information is not communicated to users of assistive technologies. Learn more about prohibited ARIA roles.

Affected elements (1):

Element 1 DAP-246a4dd6

Snippet:

<div class="progress" id="progress" aria-label="Video: " style="width: 0%;">

Label: Video:

  • aria-label attribute cannot be used on a div with no valid role attribute.

Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order) DAP-a04d28fe

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

Accessibility Findings

https://weather.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 67

Axe findings: 5

Background and foreground colors do not have a sufficient contrast ratio. (rule: color-contrast) DAP-86bc430e

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Learn how to provide sufficient color contrast.

Disabilities affected: ~2KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~2,996 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 124,816 affected page loads) ~5KPeople who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Estimated ~5,367 people potentially excluded (4.3% prevalence × 124,816 affected page loads)

Element 1 DAP-91b3be38

Element path: div.one-sixth-first > div#forecast-lookup > form#getForecast > input#inputstring

Snippet:

<input id="inputstring" name="inputstring" type="text" value="Enter location ..." onclick="this.value=''" autocomplete="off">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element has insufficient color contrast of 2.84 (foreground color: #999999, background color: #ffffff, font size: 8.0pt (10.6667px), font weight: normal). Expected contrast ratio of 4.5:1
`<html>` element does not have a `[lang]` attribute (rule: html-has-lang) DAP-0d01a89e

If a page doesn't specify a `lang` attribute, a screen reader assumes that the page is in the default language that the user chose when setting up the screen reader. If the page isn't actually in the default language, then the screen reader might not announce the page's text correctly. Learn more about the `lang` attribute.

Disabilities affected: ~1KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~1,248 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 124,816 affected page loads) ~374People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~374 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 124,816 affected page loads) ~5KPeople with cognitive, learning, or language differences. Estimated ~5,866 people potentially excluded (4.7% prevalence × 124,816 affected page loads)

Element path: html

Snippet:

<html xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1999/xhtml">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • The <html> element does not have a lang attribute
Image elements do not have `[alt]` attributes (rule: image-alt) DAP-f0437918

Informative elements should aim for short, descriptive alternate text. Decorative elements can be ignored with an empty alt attribute. Learn more about the `alt` attribute.

Disabilities affected: ~1KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. https://egov.uscis.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 90

Axe findings: 1

The document uses `<meta http-equiv="refresh">` (rule: meta-refresh) DAP-cc3b4efb

Users do not expect a page to refresh automatically, and doing so will move focus back to the top of the page. This may create a frustrating or confusing experience. Learn more about the refresh meta tag.

Disabilities affected: ~1KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~1,224 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 122,408 affected page loads) ~367People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~2,693 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 122,408 affected page loads)

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

https://nsopw.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 99

Axe findings: 1

Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order) DAP-1572b804

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

Accessibility Findings

https://i94.cbp.dhs.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 90

Axe findings: 5

`[aria-hidden="true"]` elements contain focusable descendents (rule: aria-hidden-focus) DAP-36bf93e1

Focusable descendents within an `[aria-hidden="true"]` element prevent those interactive elements from being available to users of assistive technologies like screen readers. Learn how `aria-hidden` affects focusable elements.

Disabilities affected: ~1KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~1,024 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 102,353 affected page loads) ~2KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~2,456 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 102,353 affected page loads) ~307People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. <div tabindex="0" class="cdk-visually-hidden cdk-focus-trap-anchor" aria-hidden="true">

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • Focusable content should have tabindex="-1" or be removed from the DOM

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

Some elements have a `[tabindex]` value greater than 0 (rule: tabindex) DAP-0256ac01

A value greater than 0 implies an explicit navigation ordering. Although technically valid, this often creates frustrating experiences for users who rely on assistive technologies. Learn more about the `tabindex` attribute.

Buttons do not have an accessible name (rule: button-name) DAP-d11e6ced

When a button doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it as "button", making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make buttons more accessible.

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have inner text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element does not have an implicit (wrapped) <label>
  • Element does not have an explicit <label>
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"

Links rely on color to be distinguishable. (rule: link-in-text-block) DAP-4d2fde10

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Link text that is discernible improves the experience for users with low vision. Learn how to make links distinguishable.

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Link text that is discernible improves the experience for users with low vision. Learn how to make links distinguishable.

Disabilities affected: ~1KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~1,928 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 80,344 affected page loads) ~3KPeople who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Estimated ~3,455 people potentially excluded (4.3% prevalence × 80,344 affected page loads)

Element 1 DAP-29efa714

Element path: div#alert-container > div#alert-message-box-0 > div#alert-message mnp-font > a

Snippet:

<a href="mailto:nesd@nesd-mail.onbmc.mil" target="_blank">

Label: nesd@nesd-mail.onbmc.mil

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • The link has insufficient color contrast of 2.68:1 with the surrounding text. (Minimum contrast is 3:1, link text: #1565c0, surrounding text: #212529)

  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
List items (`<li>`) are not contained within `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` parent elements. (rule: listitem) DAP-b647a33a

Screen readers require list items (`<li>`) to be contained within a parent `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` to be announced properly. Learn more about proper list structure.

Some elements have a `[tabindex]` value greater than 0 (rule: tabindex) DAP-4d2e97e5

A value greater than 0 implies an explicit navigation ordering. Although technically valid, this often creates frustrating experiences for users who rely on assistive technologies. Learn more about the `tabindex` attribute.

`button`, `link`, and `menuitem` elements do not have accessible names. (rule: aria-command-name) DAP-f6cdbd99

When an element doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it with a generic name, making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make command elements more accessible.

Buttons do not have an accessible name (rule: button-name) DAP-756f8154

When a button doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it as "button", making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make buttons more accessible.

Accessibility Findings

https://tsa.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 99

Axe findings: 1

Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order) DAP-1d6dadbd

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

Some elements have a `[tabindex]` value greater than 0 (rule: tabindex) DAP-46a39875

A value greater than 0 implies an explicit navigation ordering. Although technically valid, this often creates frustrating experiences for users who rely on assistive technologies. Learn more about the `tabindex` attribute.

`<html>` element does not have a `[lang]` attribute (rule: html-has-lang) DAP-415613a4

If a page doesn't specify a `lang` attribute, a screen reader assumes that the page is in the default language that the user chose when setting up the screen reader. If the page isn't actually in the default language, then the screen reader might not announce the page's text correctly. Learn more about the `lang` attribute.

Touch targets do not have sufficient size or spacing. (rule: target-size) DAP-ef8eddce

Touch targets with sufficient size and spacing help users who may have difficulty targeting small controls to activate the targets. Learn more about touch targets.

Element 1 DAP-bb3d58ad

Element path: body#bdyMain > div#main > div#actionbar > a

Snippet:

<a href="?note">

Label: Notes

  • Target has insufficient space to its closest neighbors. Safe clickable space has a diameter of 7.6px instead of at least 24px.

Accessibility Findings

https://store.usps.com

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 70

Axe findings: 9

Elements with an ARIA `[role]` that require children to contain a specific `[role]` are missing some or all of those required children. (rule: aria-required-children) DAP-cc50ea1e

Some ARIA parent roles must contain specific child roles to perform their intended accessibility functions. Learn more about roles and required children elements.

Snippet:

<ul class="nav-list" role="menubar">

Label: Skip Quick Tools Links Quick Tools Track a Package Informed Delivery …

`[role]`s are not contained by their required parent element (rule: aria-required-parent) DAP-81d032ab

Some ARIA child roles must be contained by specific parent roles to properly perform their intended accessibility functions. Learn more about ARIA roles and required parent element.

Background and foreground colors do not have a sufficient contrast ratio. (rule: color-contrast) DAP-f5383b9b

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Learn how to provide sufficient color contrast.

Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order) DAP-675161a0

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

Links do not have a discernible name (rule: link-name) DAP-99b30320

Link text (and alternate text for images, when used as links) that is discernible, unique, and focusable improves the navigation experience for screen reader users. Learn how to make links accessible.

List items (`<li>`) are not contained within `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` parent elements. (rule: listitem) DAP-ffa68a37

Screen readers require list items (`<li>`) to be contained within a parent `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` to be announced properly. Learn more about proper list structure.

Affected elements (3):

Element 1 DAP-6167e109

Element path: section.hero-section > div.hero-indicators > div.d-flex > button.indicator

Snippet:

<button class="indicator active" aria-label="Slide 1" data-slide="1">

Label: Slide 1

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Target has insufficient size (12px by 12px, should be at least 24px by 24px)
  • Target has insufficient space to its closest neighbors. Safe clickable space has a diameter of 16px instead of at least 24px.

ARIA input fields do not have accessible names (rule: aria-input-field-name) DAP-f8694bf1

When an input field doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it with a generic name, making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn more about input field labels.

List items (`<li>`) are not contained within `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` parent elements. (rule: listitem) DAP-4ead3bcb

Screen readers require list items (`<li>`) to be contained within a parent `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` to be announced properly. Learn more about proper list structure.

Element 1 DAP-f46efa38

Snippet:

<a href="tel:800-321-6742" onmousedown="_
                                                sendEvent('Telephone Cli…" target="_self">

Accessibility Findings

https://news.uscg.mil

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 93

Axe findings: 3

`button`, `link`, and `menuitem` elements do not have accessible names. (rule: aria-command-name) DAP-9101c5e1

When an element doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it with a generic name, making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make command elements more accessible.

Disabilities affected: ~298People who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~298 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 29,802 affected page loads) ~89People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~89 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 29,802 affected page loads) ~656People with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~656 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 29,802 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1 DAP-c7db0e4d

Element path: form#Form > div.dma-full-width > p#skip-target-holder > a#skip-target

Snippet:

<a id="skip-target" name="skip-target" class="skip" tabindex="-1" innertext="Start of main content" role="button">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order) DAP-1e3c67e1

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

Disabilities affected: ~298People who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~298 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 29,802 affected page loads) ~89People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~656 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 29,802 affected page loads)

Snippet:

Label: UPDATE 1: U.S. Coast Guard searches for missing vessel offshore Saipan

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Heading order invalid
Links do not have a discernible name (rule: link-name) DAP-306fd4df

Link text (and alternate text for images, when used as links) that is discernible, unique, and focusable improves the navigation experience for screen reader users. Learn how to make links accessible.

`[aria-*]` attributes do not have valid values (rule: aria-valid-attr-value) DAP-0e429dfb

Buttons do not have an accessible name (rule: button-name) DAP-9b302195

When a button doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it as "button", making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make buttons more accessible.

Disabilities affected: ~265People who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~265 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 26,510 affected page loads) ~80People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Snippet:

<button class="toggle-button svelte-1t22yhi uk-icon" uk-icon="icon: menu; ratio: 1.5">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have inner text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element does not have an implicit (wrapped) <label>
  • Element does not have an explicit <label>
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"
Background and foreground colors do not have a sufficient contrast ratio. (rule: color-contrast) DAP-3e77be1d

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Learn how to provide sufficient color contrast.

Estimated ~636 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 26,510 affected page loads) ~1KPeople who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Buttons do not have an accessible name (rule: button-name) DAP-025a50bd

When a button doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it as "button", making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make buttons more accessible.

Estimated ~238 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 23,772 affected page loads) ~71People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content.   • Element does not have inner text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element does not have an implicit (wrapped) <label>
  • Element does not have an explicit <label>
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"

Axe findings: 5

`[aria-*]` attributes do not match their roles (rule: aria-allowed-attr) DAP-e081d0a5

Each ARIA `role` supports a specific subset of `aria-*` attributes. Mismatching these invalidates the `aria-*` attributes. Learn how to match ARIA attributes to their roles.

Disabilities affected: ~211People who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~211 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 21,084 affected page loads) ~63People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~63 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 21,084 affected page loads) ~464People with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~464 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 21,084 affected page loads)

Affected elements (3):

Element 1 DAP-c581b385

Element path: div.field__item > div.paragraph > div.tabs-container > input#consumers

Snippet:

<input class="radiotab" name="tabs" type="radio" id="consumers" checked="checked" aria-expanded="false" data-once="tab-click">

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • ARIA attribute is not allowed: aria-expanded="false"

Element 2 DAP-3d6a6d26

Element path: div.field__item > div.paragraph > div.tabs-container > input#bankers

Snippet:

<input class="radiotab" name="tabs" type="radio" id="bankers" aria-expanded="false" data-once="tab-click">

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • ARIA attribute is not allowed: aria-expanded="false"

Element 3 DAP-16208efa

Element path: div.field__item > div.paragraph > div.tabs-container > input#analysts

Snippet:

<input class="radiotab" name="tabs" type="radio" id="analysts" aria-expanded="false" data-once="tab-click">

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • ARIA attribute is not allowed: aria-expanded="false"
Buttons do not have an accessible name (rule: button-name) DAP-454ce981

When a button doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it as "button", making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make buttons more accessible.

Disabilities affected: ~211People who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~211 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 21,084 affected page loads) ~63People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~63 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 21,084 affected page loads)

Snippet:

<button class="tbm-button" type="button">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have inner text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element does not have an implicit (wrapped) <label>
  • Element does not have an explicit <label>
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"

Background and foreground colors do not have a sufficient contrast ratio. (rule: color-contrast) DAP-19ac5c79

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Learn how to provide sufficient color contrast.

Disabilities affected: ~506People with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~506 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 21,084 affected page loads) ~907People who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Estimated ~907 people potentially excluded (4.3% prevalence × 21,084 affected page loads)

Some elements have a `[tabindex]` value greater than 0 (rule: tabindex) DAP-07fca181

A value greater than 0 implies an explicit navigation ordering. Although technically valid, this often creates frustrating experiences for users who rely on assistive technologies. Learn more about the `tabindex` attribute.

`[aria-*]` attributes do not match their roles (rule: aria-allowed-attr) DAP-3fb09f9a

Tables do not use `<caption>` instead of cells with the `[colspan]` attribute to indicate a caption. (rule: table-fake-caption) DAP-97a2bee5

Screen readers have features to make navigating tables easier. Ensuring that tables use the actual caption element instead of cells with the `[colspan]` attribute may improve the experience for screen reader users. Learn more about captions.
